body:
### Problem

## Summary

  An `nvim --embed` instance has been running orphaned (`ppid=1`) for 5+ days on macOS. 

**NB:** I have used Claude heavily for the investigation and I don't personally understand everything that's going on. I have learned a lot from this investigation though and know enough to be useful. I have left the process running, although based on the pattern of memory accumulation, I don't know if it's still in a memory accumulating state, or if it's in a suspended/zombie state. I'd be willing to run any debugging stuff that might be helpful. I had to do all of the lldb attach stuff manually anyways since it needs sudo, so I just ran what claude suggested I run.

**Observed:** the process retains channel id=1 (`mode=rpc, stream=stdio, type=ui`) in `nvim_list_chans()` even
  though its registered client pid is long dead, `nvim_list_uis()` returns 0, and fds 0/1/2 are marked `(revoked)` by the kernel. The Lua registry holds 4.83M function refs (~2.3 GB `phys_footprint`),
   99.99% of which originate from `vim.schedule_wrap` (`runtime/lua/vim/_core/editor.lua`).

  **Hypothesised but not verified:** the trigger was an unclean parent death (e.g. OS reboot interruption) that destroyed the pty via `revoke()` without an orderly EOF, and the channel-close path
  never fired because rstream's EOF detection treats `EBADF`/`EIO` from a revoked fd differently from a `read() == 0` EOF. The link between "stale channel" and "ref accumulation" is also a hypothesis
  — see "What we're not certain of" below.

  This is **not** the leak fixed by #36393 (that was a leak in the channel close path; v0.12.1 includes the fix). The bug below is upstream of close — the close path appears to never fire.


  ## Environment

  - Neovim **v0.12.1** (homebrew `nvim-macos-arm64` build)
  - macOS 26.4 (25E246), Apple Silicon
  - Process running ~6 days, originally launched as a tmux pane child of an automation daemon

  ## Directly observed state

  All values captured by attaching `lldb -p $PID` and injecting Lua via `_get_global_lstate` + `_luaL_loadstring`. The process was running and unmodified at capture time.

  ### `vim.api.nvim_list_chans()` (raw output)

  ```lua
  { {
      client = {
        attributes = { license = "Apache 2", pid = 90957, website = "https://neovim.io" },
        methods = vim.empty_dict(),
        name = "nvim-tui",
        type = "ui",
        version = {
          api_compatible = 0, api_level = 14, api_prerelease = false,
          major = 0, minor = 12, patch = 1, prerelease = false
        }
      },
      id = 1,
      mode = "rpc",
      stream = "stdio"
    }, {
      id = 2,
      mode = "bytes",
      stream = "stderr"
    } }
  ```

  ### `vim.api.nvim_list_uis()`

  ```
  {}    -- count = 0
  ```

  ### `lsof -p $PID` (relevant entries)

  ```
  fd 0    (revoked)
  fd 1    (revoked)
  fd 2    (revoked)
  fd 13u  unix /tmp/nvim.jrussell/vFmoU8/nvim.90968.0   (auto-listen socket)
  ```

  ### Kernel state

  ```
  ps -p 90968:  PPID=1 (reparented to launchd), running since 2026-04-22
                command: nvim --embed agent-changes.md

  Process pid 90957 (the registered nvim-tui client): not present in `ps`
  ```

  ### Lua registry contents

  ```
  debug.getregistry() entries:    4,845,709
    type=function:                4,833,089
      info.source == "@vim/_core/editor.lua"
      info.linedefined == 0:      4,832,519   (99.99% of functions)
  ```

  Sampling 200 of those `editor.lua:0` functions and inspecting their function-typed upvalues:

  ```
  190  → @lualine.nvim/lua/lualine/utils/utils.lua:205  (the wrapped fn passed to schedule_wrap)
    2  → vim/lsp/client.lua:468
    1  → render-markdown / lazy / which-key / etc.  (each)
  ```

  `vim/_core/editor.lua:269-276` is `vim.schedule_wrap`. Lualine's `utils.lua:202-222` is `timer_call`, which wraps a refresh callback in `vim.schedule_wrap` and uses the result as a libuv timer callback.

  ### Memory accounting

  ```
  phys_footprint:                                   2.3 GB
  phys_mem (RSS):                                   ~50 MB (rest swapped)
  collectgarbage("count") after 2x collect():       1,095,809 KB live Lua memory
  macOS `leaks`:                                    832 bytes truly leaked at C level
  heap size-class signature:
    4,848,818 non-object allocations averaging 128 bytes  (623 MB)
    13,715  × 128 KB VM_ALLOCATE regions                  (1.7 GB, mostly swapped)
  ```

  The combination — channel.client.pid pointing to a dead process, `uis = 0`, fds 0/1/2 kernel-revoked, channel record nevertheless still listed — appears not to be reachable through any documented orderly close path. **That contradiction is the strongest piece of evidence; everything below is interpretation around it.**

  ## What we're not certain of

  I want to be explicit about which conclusions are observation vs interpretation, because the path from "stale channel" to "ref accumulation" includes inferential steps:

  1. **Trigger**: the process is in this state, but I have no recording of *how* it got there. Hypothesised path (unverified): an OS reboot was interrupted while the pty was active → kernel called `revoke()` on the pty → nvim's TUI parent died as part of the reboot → embed survived because (a) it has an auto-listen socket, (b) something on the close path didn't fire on revoked-fd reads. Each link is plausible; none is verified.

  2. **Why the channel stays open**: I haven't read `src/nvim/event/rstream.c` or `src/nvim/msgpack_rpc/channel.c`. The hypothesis that revoked-fd `read()` returning `-1/EBADF` is treated differently from `read() == 0` (EOF) is a guess based on common Unix practice. A maintainer who knows that code can confirm or reject in minutes.

  3. **Why refs accumulate**: even with a stale channel, `vim.schedule_wrap` callbacks shouldn't leak refs unless something is preventing `nlua_unref_global` from being called after their execution. Hypothesised: the schedule callbacks contain UI redraw work that blocks on writes to channel 1, leaving them queued/not-executed, hence not unref'd. Tested partially with isolation reproductions (see below), which all show normal sawtooth behaviour with refs draining — so the hypothesis depends on something unique to the orphan state. Not directly verified.

  4. **Whether the channel would ever close**: observed it present after 5+ days. "Never" in the title would be extrapolation; "retains" / "is not reaped" reflects what was actually measured.

  ## Why this is distinct from #36392

  PR #36393 fixed a leak in the channel **close** path (closing socket with pending writes leaks memory). v0.11.5 / v0.12.0+ ship the fix. v0.12.1 includes it. The state above appears to be a case where the close path is never reached at all, so #36393 doesn't apply.

  ## Related issues

  - **#35107** — `:terminal` becomes laggy after long usage / multiple reconnects. justinmk's hypothesis on that issue: *"old dead channels are being waited on. Or maybe an old file descriptor or some other resource was not disposed after the client died, and is getting read/accessed, silently failing."* The state above is a concrete example of that pattern in a different code path (UI rpc rather than `:terminal` job channel). Different proximate cause, possibly the same root cause.
  - **#38456** — `--embed --listen` exits if stdio channel closes. Same `chan->detach` machinery; the requested behaviour there is what we accidentally have here, except with a leaking side-effect.
  - **#38807** — design discussion on preventing dangling embed servers. The state above is a concrete instance of the dangling-server scenario.

  ## Reproduction attempts

  **No minimal reproduction.** I have a live process exhibiting the state and have run ~60 reproduction attempts across v0.12.1 and v0.12.2 — none triggered the `uis=0` + `revoked fds` + `embed alive` + `chans contains stale id=1` combination via any orderly trigger.

  | Category | Tests | Result |
  |---|---|---|
  | `tmux kill-pane`, `kill-window`, `kill-session` (multiple variants, single & multi-pane) | 6 | embed cleanly EOFs and exits |
  | `kill -9 / -TERM / -HUP` on TUI parent / pane shell / shell grandparent | 8 | embed cleanly EOFs (one minor 0.12.1 vs 0.12.2 difference for `SIGHUP-shell`) |
  | `kill -9 / -TERM` on isolated tmux server | 3 | embed cleanly EOFs |
  | `SIGSTOP` on TUI parent / shell (frozen-parent variants, with/without follow-up SIGKILL) | 3 | embed alive but `uis=1`, refs stable |
  | Force `qall!` over remote-expr / `respawn-pane -k` / `detach-client` | 3 | embed exits or `uis=1` |
  | Multi-pane orchestrator pattern + race timings | 5 | embed alive but `uis=1` |
  | Plugin-only headless leak repro: lualine + render-markdown + treesitter + autocmd flood | 7 | bounded sawtooth, drains, no unbounded growth |
  | Python `pty.fork()` + `os.close(master_fd)` | 1 | embed cleanly EOFs |

  In every reproduction the embed either exits cleanly on EOF or stays alive with `uis=1` (UI still attached). Nothing produced the actual orphan state. **Hypothesised** (not verified): the trigger requires kernel-level fd revocation that bypasses the userland EOF path entirely — an OS-level event such as reboot interrupted while the pty was active, or a terminal application crashing in a way that triggers `revoke()` on the pty.

  ## Available data on request

  The PID 90968 process is preserved. From it I can produce on request:
  - Full `nvim_list_chans()` (above) and `nvim_list_uis()`
  - `vmmap` and `vmmap --summary` of the process
  - `lsof` complete listing
  - `lldb` thread backtraces (`thread backtrace all`)
  - Raw memory reads of channel/rstream/uvevents structs given symbol addresses
  - Further Lua registry samples (e.g. by upvalue chain, by closure source, by reachability)
  - Polled deltas (function-ref count over time, with the process running unimpeded)

  ## Diagnostic methodology (for reference)

  All Lua-level inspection is done by attaching with `lldb -p $PID` and injecting via the exported symbols `_get_global_lstate` and `_luaL_loadstring`:

  ```lldb
  expr -l c -- int luaL_loadstring(void*, const char*); int lua_pcall(void*,int,int,int);
  void* get_global_lstate(); void* L = get_global_lstate();
  luaL_loadstring(L, "<lua snippet writing to /tmp/file>"); lua_pcall(L,0,0,0)
  ```

  `_nlua_get_state` is **not** exported in this build, but `_get_global_lstate` is.


### Steps to reproduce

Unfortunately I have no idea how to reproduce this, and I don't know how it actually came about in the first place. I have a system I'm building which spawns nvim processes inside a tmux pane when a PR is opened on some repositories I'm working on, then it closes the window and panes when the PR closes. Normally this state of nvim process doesn't happen, but every now and then it does happen. It's probably happened three or four times in the last year. This is the first time I've investigated it thoroughly.

### Expected behavior

No memory leak.

### Nvim version (nvim -v)

0.12.1 release

### Vim (not Nvim) behaves the same?

No.

### Operating system/version

macOS 26.4

### Terminal name/version

tmux 3.6a inside iterm2 3.6.10

### $TERM environment variable

tmux-256color

### Installation

homebrew

comment_data.body:
what is a one-sentence description of the actual problem? Is `stale stdio rpc channel record` the primary bug?

> **Hypothesised but not verified:** the trigger was an unclean parent death (e.g. OS reboot interruption) that destroyed the pty via `revoke()` without an orderly EOF, and the channel-close path

sure, that's possible. and if it happens, then plugin code can do arbitrary things which consume memory ("unbounded vim.schedule_wrap callbacks").

Primary bug is that I have an nvim instance hanging around on my machine that just infinitely grows in RAM until I manually kill it, even though the window and session it was started in has long since died.

> It's probably happened three or four times in the last year.

There was a lot of work done in this area (channel subsystem) in the last ~2 months, so the last year is not relevant. I also suggest updating to 0.12.2 ...

## Proposal

A concrete improvement we can consider, is to add a `:checkhealth` that looks for suspicious orphan Nvim servers. That would be generally useful.
I'll see if this happens again with 0.12.2. I have already upgraded, and will launch another investigation if it happens again.

milestone.description: We don't plan to work on this, but will accept high quality contributions from someone who will own the feature and follow up on bug reports.
milestone.title: needs-owner

title: :checkhealth should report orphan Nvim servers

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
